patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v6 5/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to qspinlock

version_history.subject: [PATCH v6 5/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to qspinlock

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new tracepoint called 'contended_release' to the qspinlock implementation in the Linux kernel. The tracepoint is used to track when a contended release occurs, which means that multiple threads are trying to acquire the lock simultaneously. The patch introduces an out-of-line function for releasing the lock when tracing is enabled, which minimizes the impact on the hot path and avoids unnecessary register saves. This change ensures that the tracepoint works correctly across all architectures, including those with custom unlock implementations. The binary size increase due to this patch is estimated to be minimal, especially in configurations where the UNINLINE_SPIN_UNLOCK option is not used.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v6 4/7] locking: Factor out queued_spin_release()
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new function, `queued_spin_release()`, which is an arch-overridable unlock primitive. It replaces the existing `queued_spin_unlock()` function in various architectures, including x86 and MIPS. The change is preparatory for adding contended release tracepoint instrumentation to `queued_spin_unlock()`. No functional changes are made.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v6 3/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to sleepable locks

version_history.subject: [PATCH v6 3/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to sleepable locks

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new tracepoint called contended_release to the Linux kernel's locking subsystem. The contended_release tracepoint fires when a lock is released under contention, allowing for correlation of lock hold time with waiter events by lock address. The patch modifies the slowpath unlock paths of various sleepable locks (mutex, rtmutex, semaphore, rwsem, percpu-rwsem, and RT-specific rwbase locks) to include calls to trace_contended_release(). This enables more detailed tracing and debugging of locking behavior in the kernel.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v6 6/7] locking: Factor out __queued_read_unlock()/__queued_write_unlock()
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch refactors the code for unlocking queued read and write locks in the Linux kernel, extracting the unlock operations into separate functions (__queued_read_unlock and __queued_write_unlock) to improve maintainability and prepare for future changes.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v6 7/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to qrwlock

version_history.subject: [PATCH v6 7/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to qrwlock

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a contended_release tracepoint to queued rwlocks, allowing for tracing of lock releases when contention occurs. The implementation uses an out-of-line traced unlock approach similar to that used by queued spinlocks. This enables the collection of data on lock release behavior under contention, which can be useful for debugging and performance analysis purposes.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v6 2/7] locking/percpu-rwsem: Extract __percpu_up_read()
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch extracts the slowpath of percpu_up_read() into a new function __percpu_up_read(). The original percpu_up_read() is simplified, and the slowpath is moved to the new function. This change avoids increasing binary size due to the addition of a tracepoint in an inline function.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v6 1/7] tracing/lock: Remove unnecessary linux/sched.h include

version_history.subject: [PATCH v6 1/7] tracing/lock: Remove unnecessary linux/sched.h include

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch removes an unnecessary include statement from the lock tracing header file, as none of the trace events in this file reference anything from linux/sched.h.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v6 0/7] locking: contended_release tracepoint instrumentation
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds a new tracepoint called contended_release that fires on the lock holder's side when a lock is released while there are waiters. This provides additional information such as hold time estimation and the holder's stack at release time, which can be useful for debugging and performance analysis. The patch also addresses some issues with existing tracepoints and adds support for reader/writer locks. It includes several iterations of changes to address reviewer comments and build failures.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [LSF/MM/BPF TOPIC][RFC PATCH v4 00/27] Private Memory Nodes (w/ Compressed RAM)

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v6 0/2] mm: improve write performance with RWF_DONTCACHE

version_history.subject: [PATCH v6 0/2] mm: improve write performance with RWF_DONTCACHE

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 1/2] mm: track DONTCACHE dirty pages per bdi_writeback
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new counter to track the number of dirty pages with the dropbehind flag set, which are written via RWF_DONTCACHE. The counter is used by the writeback flusher to determine how many pages to write back when expediting writeback for IOCB_DONTCACHE writes. This allows for more efficient handling of these types of writes without flushing the entire BDI's dirty pages. The patch introduces a new enum value WB_DONTCACHE_DIRTY and updates several functions in mm/filemap.c and mm/page-writeback.c to increment or decrement this counter as necessary.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 2/2] mm: kick writeback flusher for IOCB_DONTCACHE with targeted dirty tracking
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves the performance of writeback in Linux by moving the submission of writeback work off the writer's hot path. It introduces a new flusher kick mechanism that drains dirty pages in the background, reducing lock contention and improving tail latency. The patch also adds targeted dirty tracking to limit the scope of expedited writeback and preserve I/O batching. This results in significant improvements in throughput and latency for sequential writes with IOCB_DONTCACHE enabled.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series improves write performance when using the RWF_DONTCACHE flag by addressing several bugs and optimizing the interaction between the file system and the writeback flusher. The changes include tracking dirty pages per bdi_writeback, kicking the flusher thread instead of initiating writeback inline, and adding a mechanism to run 'perf lock' around testcases. Additionally, the patch series fixes issues with concurrent readers decrementing WB_DONTCACHE_DIRTY and lost wakeups from concurrent DONTCACHE writers.

patches_acked.subject: Re: [PATCH 0/6] SUNRPC: Address remaining cache_check_rcu() UAF in cache content files

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v4] mm/mempolicy: track user-defined mempolicy allocations

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v6 00/22] Virtual Swap Space

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 01/22] mm/swap: decouple swap cache from physical swap infrastructure
series_items.patch_summary: This patch decouples the swap cache from physical swap infrastructure to prepare for virtualizing swap space. The swap cache will be managed at the virtual swap layer, and a single global lock is temporarily used to synchronize swap cache accesses. The swap table infrastructure is repurposed to serve as the rmap (physical -> virtual swap mapping) later. This patch removes swap cache related helpers from the swap table and prepares for future changes in the swap cache management.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 02/22] swap: rearrange the swap header file
series_items.patch_summary: This patch rearranges the swap header file (include/linux/swap.h) to group related functions into categories such as lifecycle swap, swap cache, and physical swap allocator and device APIs. The extern keyword is removed from these functions. This change is a clean-up effort with no functional changes intended.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 03/22] mm: swap: add an abstract API for locking out swapoff
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces an abstract API to lock out swapoff operations in the Linux kernel. The current implementation of locking out swapoff relies on getting a reference to the backing swap device, which is not suitable for virtual swap designs where the swap entry may not have a backing device or its backend can change during its lifetime. The new API, provided by two inline functions `tryget_swap_entry` and `put_swap_entry`, allows for generic locking out of swapoff operations without requiring knowledge of the underlying swap device. This patch modifies several kernel files to use this new API, ensuring that swapoff is properly locked out in various scenarios.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 04/22] zswap: add new helpers for zswap entry operations
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds new helper functions to abstract away zswap entry operations, making it easier to re-implement these functions when swap is virtualized. The helpers include storing, loading, erasing, and checking if a zswap entry is empty. These functions are used in various places throughout the codebase, such as in the zswap_writeback_entry function. By introducing these abstractions, the patch aims to improve maintainability and flexibility of the zswap implementation.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 05/22] mm/swap: add a new function to check if a swap entry is in swap cached.
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new function called is_swap_cached to check if a swap entry is in the swap cache. The function abstracts away the current implementation of checking the swap cached state, which will soon be managed at the virtual swap layer. It minimizes overhead by directly accessing the swap map using READ_ONCE. This change aims to simplify and future-proof the code for upcoming changes in swap cache management.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 06/22] mm: swap: add a separate type for physical swap slots
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new type, swp_slot_t, to represent physical swap slots on swap devices. It separates the logical view of swap entries from their physical backing state, allowing for future virtualization of swap. The mapping between the two types is an identity mapping, and the only intentional behavioral change is in swap_vma_readahead(), which now pins readahead entries via tryget_swap_entry()/put_swap_entry(). This patch is a mechanical refactor to prepare for dynamic allocation of virtual swap slots and association with backing stores.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 07/22] mm: create scaffolds for the new virtual swap implementation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds scaffolding code for virtual swap implementation, including a new source file mm/vswap.c and modifications to the Makefile and other files. The new code sets up a debugfs directory for virtual swap but does not introduce any behavioral changes yet. It also adds Johannes Weiner as a reviewer for virtual swap development.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 08/22] zswap: prepare zswap for swap virtualization
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prepares the zswap code for swap virtualization by removing its dependency on the range partition logic and replacing it with a simple unified zswap tree. The changes include updating the zswap.h header file to remove unused functions, modifying the swapfile.c file to remove calls to these functions, and updating the zswap.c file to use the new unified zswap tree. This patch is part of a larger series that aims to virtualize swap space in Linux.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 09/22] mm: swap: allocate a virtual swap slot for each swapped out page
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new virtual swap slot design for each swapped out page in the Linux kernel. It dynamically allocates a virtual slot and associates it with a physical swap slot on a swapfile or partition. The virtual swap slot is stored in page table entries and used to index into swap data structures, replacing the old physical swap slot. This change also updates the freeing ordering of swap slots and repurposes the swap table infrastructure as a reverse map for looking up virtual swap slots from their associated physical swap slots.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 10/22] swap: move swap cache to virtual swap descriptor
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the swap cache and workingset shadow for anonymous pages to the virtual swap descriptor, effectively range-partitioning the swap cache by virtual swap clusters of PMD size. This change eliminates lock contention in the swap cache. The patch modifies several kernel files, including huge_memory.c, migrate.c, shmem.c, swap.h, swap_state.c, vmscan.c, and vswap.c.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 11/22] zswap: move zswap entry management to the virtual swap descriptor
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves zswap entry management from a dedicated tree to the virtual swap descriptor, eliminating lock contention between zswap entries and reducing complexity in the codebase. The change involves modifying several files, including include/linux/zswap.h, mm/vswap.c, and mm/zswap.c, where new functions are added for storing, loading, and erasing zswap entries from the swap descriptor. The patch also includes a re-partitioning of the zswap pool by virtual swap cluster to improve performance. Overall, this change aims to simplify the code and reduce contention in the zswap system.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 12/22] swap: implement the swap_cgroup API using virtual swap
series_items.patch_summary: This patch implements the swap_cgroup API using virtual swap, which allows for dynamic allocation of memory overhead for swap cgroup information. This reduces the memory overhead in large but sparsely used swap spaces by only allocating memory when needed. The patch removes static arrays and instead stores swap entries' cgroup information in the swap descriptor. It also introduces a new function to record swap cgroup information, __swap_cgroup_record, which is called from the virtual swap cluster allocation code.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 13/22] swap: manage swap entry lifecycle at the virtual swap layer
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the management of swap entry lifecycle to the virtual swap layer, adding two new fields to the swap descriptor: in_swapcache and swap count. It re-implements various swap entry lifecycle APIs in the virtual swap layer, simplifying logic by not implementing swap count continuation for now.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 14/22] mm: swap: decouple virtual swap slot from backing store
series_items.patch_summary: This patch decouples virtual swap slots from their backing storage, allowing them to be backed by physical swap slots, zero swap pages, compressed objects in the zswap pool, or in-memory pages. This change enables the use of zswap and zero swap page optimization without reserving a slot on a swapfile or using a swapfile at all, resulting in significant disk space savings for hosts with high memory usage. The patch also introduces new requirements for batched swap operations to ensure that entries have consistent backing states.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 15/22] zswap: do not start zswap shrinker if there is no physical swap slots
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prevents the zswap shrinker from starting if there are no physical swap slots available, which is a problem when swap is virtualized and pre-allocated slots on swapfile for each zswap entry are not used.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 16/22] swap: do not unnecessarily pin readahead swap entries
series_items.patch_summary: This patch optimizes swap readahead by avoiding unnecessary pinning of swap entries in the same virtual swap cluster as the target entry. The patch introduces a new function `vswap_same_cluster` to check if two virtual swap entries belong to the same cluster, and uses this function to skip pinning entries that are already protected by the pinned target entry. This reduces overhead and improves performance.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 17/22] swapfile: remove zeromap bitmap
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes the zeromap bitmap from the swapfile code in the Linux kernel. The zeromap bitmap was used to track zero pages, but since zero swap entries are now treated as a separate backend in the virtual swap layer, this bitmap is no longer needed. Removing it saves memory overhead by reducing the number of bits required per swap page. This change does not affect the behavior of the system and only optimizes memory usage.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 18/22] memcg: swap: only charge physical swap slots
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's memory cgroup (memcg) swap handling to only charge physical swap slots, rather than virtual ones. This change is made possible by the removal of zswap and zero-filled swap page optimization from using physical swap space. The patch also optimizes IDR lookups on hot paths by converting memcg swap charge/uncharge/clear APIs to take a struct mem_cgroup pointer directly. Additionally, it reduces IDR lookups in several functions, including vswap_alloc_swap_slot(), vswap_free(), and memcg1_swapin(). Overall, this patch aims to improve performance and accuracy of memory cgroup swap handling.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 19/22] swap: simplify swapoff using virtual swap
series_items.patch_summary: This patch simplifies and optimizes the swapoff process by utilizing virtual swap slots stored at page table entries. It eliminates the need for a page table walk, instead iterating through allocated swap slots on the swapfile to fault in corresponding virtual swap slots. This approach is cleaner and slightly more performant, reducing kernel CPU time by about 13% compared to the original code. The patch modifies several files, including mm/swapfile.c, which has undergone significant changes to implement this new design.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 20/22] swapfile: replace the swap map with bitmaps
series_items.patch_summary: This patch replaces the swap map in Linux's swapfile code with two bitmaps, one for tracking allocated states and another for bad states. This change is made possible by moving the swap count state to the virtual swap layer, which reduces the number of bits required per swap entry from 6 to 1. The patch updates the swap_info_struct structure in include/linux/swap.h and modifies several functions in mm/swapfile.c to use the new bitmap-based approach.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 21/22] vswap: batch contiguous vswap free calls
series_items.patch_summary: This patch optimizes the vswap_free() function by batching contiguous free calls to reduce overhead on non-disk-swap backends, where releasing and reacquiring the cluster lock is unnecessary. The patch modifies the release_vswap_slot() function to take an additional argument for the number of slots to be released, allowing multiple frees to be batched together. This change improves performance by minimizing the number of lock acquisitions and releases.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 22/22] vswap: cache cluster lookup
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a per-CPU cache to store the most recently used vswap cluster during vswap operations, allowing consecutive lookups on the same cluster to skip traversing the global xarray. This improves performance for swap-heavy workloads by reducing the cost of xa_load operations. The cache is cleared when a lookup targets a different cluster, the cached cluster becomes empty, or the CPU goes offline. A reference is held on the cached cluster to keep it alive.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series introduces virtual swap space, a new design for managing swap memory that aims to reduce CPU and memory overhead on fast swap backends like zram and PMEM. The patches implement a cluster-based allocation algorithm, re-use the swap table as a reverse map, and dynamically allocate virtual swap slots for each swapped-out page.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham
review_comments.summary: The reviewer is waiting for feedback from Kairui on the regressions reported in [19]. They mention that their setup cannot be 100% similar to Kairui's and are sending it out for his feedback.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham
review_comments.summary: The reviewer is implementing a cluster-based allocation algorithm for virtual swap slots, inspired by Kairui Song and Chris Li's implementation. They are also re-using the swap table as a reverse map.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham
review_comments.summary: The reviewer is dynamically allocating virtual swap slots for each swapped-out page and associating them with physical swap slots. They are also repurposing the swap table infrastructure as a reverse map.
